content: When you first encounter the concept of in-memory I/O in Rust, it is easy to conflate the standard library's `io::Cursor` with generic memory buffers like `Vec<u8>`. While `Vec<u8>` serves as a raw container for bytes, it lacks the behavioral contract required by functions expecting streams. The `io::Cursor` struct acts as a bridge, implementing the `Read`, `Write`, and `Seek` traits so that any code designed to read from a file or network socket can operate on your heap-allocated buffer without modification. This is particularly useful when unit-testing I/O-heavy logic, such as deserializing a custom binary format or parsing a protocol, because you want to avoid spawning actual OS processes just to verify your parser handles edge cases correctly. The core distinction lies in how `Cursor` interacts with memory: it does not allocate new storage on its own; instead, it wraps an existing buffer and manages the position index within that buffer using a mutable reference or ownership transfer. When you pass a `Vec<u8>` to `io::Cursor`, the cursor internally holds a `&mut [u8]` pointing to your vector, allowing operations like `read_exact` or `write_all` to mutate the underlying data directly without copying, which is critical for performance in tight loops where memory allocation would become a bottleneck. To modify the buffer from within a function that expects an I/O stream, you must ensure the cursor owns the reference or the vector it wraps; attempting to use `get_ref` on a cursor wrapping a non-mutable shared reference will fail because Rust's ownership system prevents simultaneous mutable access and immutable borrowing of the same data. The most common pattern involves creating a cursor with your vector, performing read/write operations via trait methods, and then extracting the final state using either `into_inner` if you own the vector or `get_ref` if you only need an immutable view to inspect the modified bytes. From a performance perspective, `Cursor<Vec<u8>>` is often faster than passing a raw slice because it guarantees that all I/O trait methods are implemented correctly for the specific buffer type, whereas manual slicing requires careful handling of bounds checking and potential invalidation risks. The idiomatic Rust approach here avoids unnecessary clones or moves by leveraging the fact that `Vec::into_iter` can be used to consume the vector into a cursor in one operation, or conversely, using `Cursor::new_mut` to get a mutable handle before writing data back into the buffer. This abstraction allows you to build generic binary serializers where the output is not necessarily written to disk immediately but accumulated in memory for later transmission or storage, effectively decoupling serialization logic from I/O concerns while maintaining strict type safety and zero-cost abstractions.
